title: BoltDB backed state implementation
body:
This adds a BoltDB-backed state implementation for libpod, and the ability to select state implementations when creating libpod instances.

I have made boltdb the default backing state implementation for now, though we need some performance data to decide what the actual default should be.

Still needs unit testing, which I am working on. Once that's done, should be merge-ready.

This is a breaking change to database storage, and will break all your containers after it is merged.

comments.body: Also needs the ability to verify DB config against runtime config like the SQL state presently can
DB config verification and unit tests added. This should theoretically be passing tests. Removing WIP label.
Tests are green, @rhatdan PTAL

@runcom Could you do a review of this patch set.  

I would like to see us eventually remove sqllite, if we can get the same performance.   But for now I understand keeping it.
/cc @mtrmac PTAL as well
:umbrella: The latest upstream changes (presumably e6be800) made this pull request unmergeable. Please resolve the merge conflicts.
Once #220 merges I am going to carve this up so everything except the boltdb state can land. This will make it easier to review, and allow pod development to continue independently of this merging.
:umbrella: The latest upstream changes (presumably 2e48c60) made this pull request unmergeable. Please resolve the merge conflicts.
:umbrella: The latest upstream changes (presumably 4d70a69) made this pull request unmergeable. Please resolve the merge conflicts.

I have a high level question - is the intention that since libpod is an implementation detail of kube, which maintains its own state (etcd, or even a higher level tool like <https://github.com/box/kube-applier>), the database might change again in the future? 

In other words is this just basically a transient cache?  Or are we really trying to do something more "persistent" here?

Libpod should be able to act as a container runtime independent of Kube. The DB should act as persistent storage to fulfill that role - we can't guarantee we have something above us that will remember containers if we forget. For the podman usecase, we don't even have a persistent daemon, so we need to cache more state to disk than Docker and CRI-O do right now, which makes the DB implementation more important than either of those. Unfortunately, we're also making breaking changes to it on an almost daily basis (we tried to do a big batch add of everything we thought we'd need last week, but managed to miss a lot). BoltDB should ease the burden of schema changes in the DB while we're still in heavy development. Eventually I'd like DB type to be an exposed config knob that people can fiddle with to suit their specific configuration.

Squashed and rebased on top of #229 

#229 contains all code previous in this PR that is not directly related to the BoltDB state implementation

:umbrella: The latest upstream changes (presumably ecb74aa) made this pull request unmergeable. Please resolve the merge conflicts.
@mheon Needs a rebase, and is it time to merge this and start testing performance?
@rhatdan Think we ought to merge #268 first to get the last few functions we need in the state. I'd also like to address a few of the comments @mtrmac to try and improve our read performance

Pushed new version. Added pod functions. Not quite done yet (not passing all state conformance unit tests). Also want to make a switch away from JSON encoding before we merge, so we don't have a breaking encoding change.

Hopefully passes tests now.

Still needs some changes to be merge ready, but it's getting close.

Alright, this should be ready for final review/merge before public release. There are still things I want to do, but the on-disk DB format should be finalized, so any future changes won't break compatability.

@rhatdan PTAL
Other then the question on errors.  LGTM
@baude @umohnani8 @TomSweeneyRedHat PTAL
lgtm
